BEARING 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | November, 1996

School League, Winter O' Series to Begin

Know any students in 1st through 12th grade? Get them involved in the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League. The league will begin a new season on December 21 at Ft. Steilacoom Park in Tacoma and will conclude with the championship meet at Hamlin Park in Seattle. There will also be two practice meets in early December (see schedule for more specifics). For more information or to receive a brochure, call Debbie Newell at 488-7471.

While the school league participants are rushing headlong through the underbrush, you older types will have a change to test yourselves as well. The winter O' series also begins at Ft. Steilacoom Park in Tacoma and continues through February 28 (see schedule for other meets in the series). Scoring will be by place (100 pts for 1st, 95 for 2nd, 92 for 3rd, 90 for 4th, 89 for 5th, etc.) Your best 4 scores will be totaled to determine the male and female winners.

If you are able to help with any of the event, especially with vetting courses in the early morning, please call Debbie at 488-7471.

☁️ ❄️ ❄️ ❄️

COC ANNUAL MEETING RESCHEDULED!

Due to difficult weather and driving conditions, the COC annual meeting is rescheduled for Tuesday, December 3 at 6pm.

The meeting will include dinner (regular menu prices) and is at Coco's restaurant located on 205th between I-5 and Hwy 99. Go west from I-5 at exit #177 (Hwy 104). Continue west until you see Coco's; you will need to bear left at the fork in order to gain access to the parking. (This is not the one we used last year in Lake Forest Park.)

As usual, the meeting will include a brief election, presentation of awards, some entertainment and fun activities, and a change to meet orienteering friends in a warm and dry environment.

COC Presents Awards

At it's annual meeting, Cascade Orienteering Club will present awards to two members who have done a lot of work and made large contributions to the club.

The Board of Directors' Life Service Award was presented to Ardis Dull for her many years of distinguished work for the club, particularly her long stint as Secretary. Ron Brown was presented the President's Award for his outstanding service as volunteer coordinator over the past year. Congratulations to Ardis and Ron. Their contributions have made a big difference to the club and to orienteering in the Pacific Northwest.

Bearing 315 | November, 1996 | Page 2

Minnesota Veteran's World Cup Weather

Don Atkinson

As I watched the weather maps this fall, I occasionally checked out the conditions over Minnesota since I was planning to attend the VWC meets there in late September and early October next year. I was surprised to see that there were more frequent radar rain echoes there than over the Seattle area.

My preconception had been that the weather there would have been better than here. To verify if this fall was the normal situation, or just a statistical quirk, I checked into the averages for Brainerd and Little Falls. As background, Seattle, along with most of the area west of the Rockies, has a winter precipitation maximum and a summer drought. However, east of the Rockies, most areas have a summer precipitation maximum and less in winter. So in Seattle a date later in the fall has a higher chance of rain, but in Minnesota, the later dates have a lower chance of rain (or snow!).

Here are the averages:

Max. Temp. Min. Temp. Precip (for Month)
Brainerd
Sept. 68 43 3.12
Oct. 56 33 2.37
VWC interpolation 62 38 2.80
Little Falls
Sept. 71 47 2.87
Oct. 59 36 2.49
VWC interpolation 65 41 2.70

For comparison, here are our local averages, for the Montlake recording station:
Sept. 69 53 1.95
Oct. 60 47 3.25
VWC interpolation 65 50 2.60

Conclusions: Minnesota is warmer in the summer and colder in winter and has a wider daily temperature range than Seattle, but VWC happens to be scheduled at the crossover point where the average conditions are fairly similar here and there. Expect significantly colder nights at VWC than at the same period in Seattle, but otherwise, conditions are similar. However, we are used to water features often being dry in the early fall, but there, if is is mapped blue then it probably will be full. The probability of snow is very low, but not zero.

Field Notes

  • The USOF Board Executive Committee has approved Cascade's bid for the 1997 USOF Convention. The convention will be on August 25-26, and will be held at Camp Waskowitz, a semi-rustic camp near North Bend. Rick Hood has agreed to be the convention director. The convention is planned to be preceded by an two-day meet on August 23-24, probably at Trollhaugen. More details about the convention will be in upcoming issues of Bearing 315.
  • Wanted: Vetters. Job Description: run school league courses on the day of the event to identify and correct potential problems. Compensation: thanks from the competitors (and maybe a donut if you're lucky). Contact: Debbie Newell, 488-7471
  • Congratulations to Eric Bone on placing third among men at the US Champs this year, against very strong competition.
  • After four years, Peter Golde is looking to step down as editor of Bearing 315. If you are interested in become editor or co-editor, please contact Margie Bone at 634-2279

[Please send your information, announcements, rumors, gossip, or anything else about orienteering or orienteers to Field Notes. I can't print it if I don't know about it. E-mail: petergo@microsoft.com]

Bearing 315 | November, 1996 | Page 3

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event Fees

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.
Club members (any club) $4.00
Non-club members $5.00
Junior (under 18) $3.00
Families map fee only after first two

Meet Director: Don Atkinson
Courses: Don Denbo
Volunteers: Mark Bingham, Ross Stevens, Ken Lew.
Marker Pickup: Don Denbo, Don Atkinson, Bill Cusworth, Nick Bone.

The weather forecast was pessimistic but actual weather was good with showers moving in only as we finished picking up the markers. Some competitors on course 3 could not find marker #6, a deep pit. The meet director and course setter checked the area after the event and concluded that the large deep pit was at the mapped location, although an unmapped small pit about 40 meters NW of the mapped pit was a confusing factor. The marker had been hung high above the pit edge to compensate for the "greening" of the "open" forest as the small alders grew taller. This growth made the mapped vegetation boundary useless as a guide to locating the marker and probably contributed to the difficulty.

I had to go out of town that weekend so my wife and our two-week old son volunteered as meet directors. Sacajawea's last meet of the year is at Horn Rapids County Park on Sunday, December 1 (note new date) at 1:00. — Frank Skorina

The rules are that no two markers in the field with the same control discription (a vegetation boundary in this case) can be closer than 100m. In light of this the meet director feels it is not appropriate to disqualify anyone.

Bearing 315 | November, 1996 | Page 6

Farrel McWhirter: Course 3 and 4

Course 3/4 introduced a unique form of orienteering. Each control was assigned a point value, but the participant didn't know ahead of time what that value was. The goal was to punch a combination of controls (in any order, but at most once per control) whose point values added up to exactly 25 points. We were told that controls were worth 0 (yes, 0!), 1, 2, or 3 points, but not how many controls of each value were out there. Some people punched an unfortunate combination early in the course only to find that they could not get exactly 25 points and had to pay the penalty (10 minutes per point over 25, 30 minutes per point under 25).

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Bearing 315
is published by:

School League, Winter O' Series to Begin

Know any students in 1st through 12th grade? Get them involved in the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League. The league will begin a new season on December 21 at Ft. Steilacoom Park in Tacoma and will conclude with the championship meet at Hamlin Park in Seattle. There will also be two practice meets in early December (see schedule for more specifics). For more information or to receive a brochure, call Debbie Newell at 488-7471.

While the school league participants are rushing headlong through the underbrush, you older types will have a change to test yourselves as well. The winter O' series also begins at Ft. Steilacoom Park in Tacoma and continues through February 28 (see schedule for other meets in the series). Scoring will be by place (100 pts for 1st, 95 for 2nd, 92 for 3rd, 90 for 4th, 89 for 5th, etc.) Your best 4 scores will be totaled to determine the male and female winners.
If you are able to help with any of the event, especially with vetting courses in the early morning, please call Debbie at 488-7471.
Due to difficult weather and driving conditions, the COC annual meeting is rescheduled for Tuesday, December 3 at 6pm.

The meeting will include dinner (regular menu prices) and is at Coco's restaurant located on 205th between I-5 and Hwy 99. Go west from I-5 at exit #177 (Hwy 104). Continue west until you see Coco's; you will need to bear left at the fork in order to gain access to the parking. (This is not the one we used last year in Lake Forest Park.)
As usual, the meeting will include a brief election, presentation of awards, some entertainment and fun activities, and a change to meet orienteering friends in a warm and dry environment.
At it's annual meeting, Cascade Orienteering Club will present awards to two members who have done a lot of work and made large contributions to the club.

The Board of Directors' Life Service Award was presented to Ardis Dull for her many years of distinguished work for the club, particularly her long stint as Secretary. Ron Brown was presented the President's Award for his outstanding service as volunteer coordinator over the past year. Congratulations to Ardis and Ron. Their contributions have made a big difference to the club and to orienteering in the Pacific Northwest.
School League, Winter O' Series to Begin ............................ 1

As I watched the weather maps this fall, I occasionally checked out the conditions over Minnesota since I was planning to attend the VWC meets there in late September and early October next year. I was surprised to see that there were more frequent radar rain echoes there than over the Seattle area.

My preconception had been that the weather there would have been better than here. To verify if this fall was the normal situation, or just a statistical quirk, I checked into the averages for Brainerd and Little Falls. As background, Seattle, along with most of the area west of the Rockies, has a winter precipitation maximum and a summer drought. However, east of the Rockies, most areas have a summer precipitation maximum and less in winter. So in Seattle a date later in the fall has a higher chance of rain, but in Minnesota, the later dates have a lower chance of rain (or snow!).
For comparison, here are our local averages, for the Montlake recording station:

Conclusions: Minnesota is warmer in the summer and colder in winter and has a wider daily temperature range than Seattle, but VWC happens to be scheduled at the crossover point where the average conditions are fairly similar here and there. Expect significantly colder nights at VWC than at the same period in Seattle, but otherwise, conditions are similar. However, we are used to water features often being dry in the early fall, but there, if is is mapped blue then it probably will be full. The probability of snow is very low, but not zero.

The USOF Board Executive Committee has approved Cascade's bid for the 1997 USOF Convention. The convention will be on August 25-26, and will be held at Camp Waskowitz, a semi-rustic camp near North Bend. Rick Hood has agreed to be the convention director. The convention is planned to be preceded by an two-day meet on August 23-24, probably at Trollhaugen. More details about the convention will be in upcoming issues of Bearing 315.

Wanted: Vetters. Job Description: run school league courses on the day of the event to identify and correct potential problems. Compensation: thanks from the competitors (and maybe a donut if you're lucky). Contact: Debbie Newell, 488-7471
Congratulations to Eric Bone on placing third among men at the US Champs this year, against very strong competition.
After four years, Peter Golde is looking to step down as editor of Bearing 315. If you are interested in become editor or co-editor, please contact Margie Bone at 634-2279

[Please send your information, announcements, rumors, gossip, or anything else about orienteering or orienteers to Field Notes. I can't print it if I don't know about it. E-mail: petergo@microsoft.com]
Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.
map fee only after first two
Meet Director, Courses: Ken Klepsch Timing, Registration: Kohanne Klepsch
Cle Elum, WA Cascade Orienteering Club September 21, 1996
Meet Director: Don Atkinson Courses: Don Denbo Volunteers: Mark Bingham, Ross Stevens, Ken Lew. Marker Pickup: Don Denbo, Don Atkinson, Bill Cusworth, Nick Bone.

The weather forecast was pessimistic but actual weather was good with showers moving in only as we finished picking up the markers. Some competitors on course 3 could not find marker #6, a deep pit. The meet director and course setter checked the area after the event and concluded that the large deep pit was at the mapped location, although an unmapped small pit about 40 meters NW of the mapped pit was a confusing factor. The marker had been hung high above the pit edge to compensate for the "greening" of the "open" forest as the small alders grew taller. This growth made the mapped vegetation boundary useless as a guide to locating the marker and probably contributed to the difficulty.
Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteering Club October 13, 1996

For the first time in many weeks, significant rain fell, which resulted in only the die hards and a couple of dogs braving the elements. Nobody was permanently lost signifying a successful meet. Frank Skorina was Course Setter & Meet Director. Ivar Husa helped with Start and Control Pickup. Holly Howard helped with finish.
Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteering Club Sunday, November 10, 1996
Meet Director: Holly Howard and Russell Skorina Timing: Russell Skorina Course Setter: Frank Skorina Control Pickup: Michel Billaux

Foggy and chilly, the perfect weather for a runnable, tree-challenged area. We had our best turnout this fall, with the majority of the participants tackling the beginner's course and driving an hour from the Tri-Cities to the meet. I had to go out of town that weekend so my wife and our two-week old son volunteered as meet directors. Sacajawea's last meet of the year is at Horn Rapids County Park on Sunday, December 1 (note new date) at 1:00. — Frank Skorina
Whidbey Island Cascade Orienteering Club October 13, 1996
Meet Director: Paul Magnus Courses, Map Update: Jim Siscel
Meet Staff: Eric Danilson, Maureen Traxler, Blythe Oraker, Joan Maza, Larry Farrar Marker Pickup: Kevin Welton, Don Atkinson, Jim Siscel

There were several mispunches on course 2 (the scout course), but no one was disqualified. The rules are that no two markers in the field with the same control discription (a vegetation boundary in this case) can be closer than 100m. In light of this the meet director feels it is not appropriate to disqualify anyone.
Redmond, WA Cascade Orienteering Club April 6, 1996
Meet Director: Mike Fritz Courses: Vivien Fritz

Course 3/4 introduced a unique form of orienteering. Each control was assigned a point value, but the participant didn't know ahead of time what that value was. The goal was to punch a combination of controls (in any order, but at most once per control) whose point values added up to exactly 25 points. We were told that controls were worth 0 (yes, 0!), 1, 2, or 3 points, but not how many controls of each value were out there. Some people punched an unfortunate combination early in the course only to find that they could not get exactly 25 points and had to pay the penalty (10 minutes per point over 25, 30 minutes per point under 25).

As a U.S. Citizen or U.S. Resident, I hereby apply for USOF membership. I understand that I am entitled to a one-year subscription to Orienteering North America, voting rights, and membership in the IOF. Please print.
If family membership, list other persons:
Member of a chartered club? Club _______________________
[ ] Individual $25 member of a chartered club

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
